entries.title: Sophia Towne stars as Nazareth wins first IHSA Class 4A state championship: 'She just won't lose'
entries.summary: <p>On Nazareth’s first possession after halftime, Roadrunners junior guard Sophia Towne caught the ball on the left side of the court with her feet firmly on the IHSA logo.</p><p>As soon as the ball hit Towne’s fingertips, she immediately went into her shooting motion and knocked down a three. Towne was in complete control on Saturday with her scoring and passing. She manipulated the defense to her liking on Saturday. Her ability to draw in a defense to open up a passing window for her teammates is particularly impressive.</p><p>She plays with a flair that elicits audible reactions from the crowds. And when her shots are falling, or her teammates are thriving, there's always a wide grin on her face.</p><p>Towne had a lot to smile about after her Roadrunners were dominant in their 55-23 IHSA Class 4A state championship win over Loyola. It's the Roadrunners' first 4A state championship — they're a 3A school.</p><p>Towne told her coach, Ed Stritzel, that they weren’t losing, and she fulfilled her promise with a 17-point, five-assist performance that powered Nazareth.</p><p>“We've been waiting all year for this,” Towne said. “We were so close [last season], so we always just [being in the state championship] in the back of our minds. ‘Peak in March’ is what we always say, and once the time came, we all knew we were not losing this game.”</p><p>Nazareth star forward Stella Sakalas drew the headlines all season, but she was plagued by foul trouble all night. Stritzel has been adamant that the Roadrunners are more than a one-woman team, and it showed in the biggest game of the season.</p><p>Freshman standout Mia Gage finished with 11 points, five rebounds and five steals. Junior forward Sam Austin scored seven points and grabbed 10 rebounds. Senior guard Lyla Shelton added eight points.</p><p>Loyola had no answer for Towne off the dribble. She’s a confident player who only needs a sliver of daylight to knock down a shot. After Sakalas drew her third foul with 3:58 left in the first half, Towne continued to lead the charge.</p><p>“She just won’t lose,” Stritzel said. “[All day], she was like, ‘We’re bringing this home.’”</p><p>Sakalas is one of the best players in the state and has proved it all season. Her getting into foul trouble would be a death sentence for most teams, but not for the Roadrunners. Not only did they maintain the lead, they added to it. They didn’t falter or waver; instead, they asserted their dominance. instead they asserted their dominance.</p><p>“Loyola is a really good team, but I think we showed everybody in the state that we're the best team in Illinois,” Stritzel said.</p><p>Whether it was Gage picking off passes and going coast-to-coast, Towne knocking down deep threes and assisting her teammates, or junior forward Sam Austin being a force on the glass, Nazareth (34-3) had many contributors in the wire-to-wire win.</p><p>After allowing 11 points to Loyola in the first quarter, the Roadrunners held the Ramblers to two, four and six points in the second, third and fourth quarters. Gage led the charge in holding Loyola to its lowest scoring output of the season with her defensive activity.</p><p>“I wanted this for my seniors so bad ... just as bad as they wanted it,” Gage said.</p><p>With a 4A title win under their belts, the Roadrunners aren’t going anywhere with Towne, Austin and Gage returning next season.</p><p>“The second half of the season prepared us for these moments,” Towne said. “When the time comes, if you want to win, you want to win."<br></p>
entries.author: Kyle Williams

entries.title: White Sox' Grant Taylor pleased with his start, although he's more of a finisher
entries.summary: <p>PEORIA, Ariz. — Grant Taylor twirled the opening inning of Saturday’s game against the Mariners and said he walked away feeling pretty good about himself.</p><p>“I feel great. I feel amazing,” he said after allowing a hit and facing four hitters. “My body is in a really good spot. I’ve done a lot of work with the pitching coaches and the staff since I got here.”</p><p>Taylor is one of the Sox’ back-end relievers, but he also opened a few games last year. Both are roles Taylor said he’s comfortable with.</p><p>“I’m used to it,” he said. “I’ve thrown late in some games during the spring. I haven’t seen a lot of big league guys, so it was nice to face some guys I’ll probably see during the season.”</p><p>During the 2025 season with the Sox, Taylor started twice and finished 10 games, saving six. With as many starting arms in camp this spring, the need for an opener could decrease. Last season, when he started twice, Taylor pitched only the first inning in each outing.</p><p>“We have a lot of really good options this year,” Taylor, 23, said. “We’ll see how that shakes out.”</p><p>Saturday’s start was to simulate both an inning early and an inning late a game.</p><p>“I’m not in on those meetings of what actually the focus was,” Taylor said.</p><h3><b>More cuts</b></h3><p>The Sox made two more camp cuts on Saturday before their game against the Mariners at the Peoria Sports Complex.</p><p>They optioned right-hander Tanner McDougal to Triple-A Charlotte and assigned left-hander Noah Schultz to minor-league camp. With these moves, they have 55 players remaining in camp: 28 pitchers, five catchers, 11 infielders and 11 outfielders.</p><p>“It’s that time of camp where guys have to get their work in,” Sox manager Will Venable said. “Those guys were outstanding. They both did a great job. We talked earlier in camp about using this as a foundation for a healthy and productive season, and they did a great job doing that.”</p><p>McDougal and Schultz combined to pitch 5.1 scoreless innings against the D-backs on Thursday at Salt River Fields. McDougal threw three innings, allowed two hits and struck out two. Schultz followed with 2.1 hitless innings, also recording two strikeouts.</p><p>“We saw what they did on the mound,” Venable said. “For two young guys to be out there and have that presence and the feel for the zone with their pitches was great to see.”</p><h3><b>Drew Thorpe update</b></h3><p>The right-hander said he weathered Friday’s second throwing session off the mound much better than his first one earlier in week. The next day following the first one, he said he suffered some soreness. Not so this time.</p><p>“It feels much better,” he said on Saturday.</p><p>Thorpe underwent Tommy John ligament replacement surgery last April and is in a slow progression to return within 12 to 18 months of the procedure.</p><h3><b>Sox 5, Mariners 1</b></h3><p><b>Davis Martin </b>pitched a nifty three innings of scoreless one-hit, four-strikeout ball following Grant Taylor’s pretty clean first. Martin also walked one. Martin, who’s certainly in line for maanger Max Venable’s starting rotation, threw 50 pitches.</p><p>“I just got back into the groove a little bit,” Martin said. “It’s just going out there and competing. Don’t make it any more than it is. Yeah, I had a lot of fun.”</p><p>This was his third outing this spring, but his first in relief after the opener.</p><p><b>Andrew Benintendi </b>started on Saturday for the second time this week after recovering from a sore right side. He went 2-for-4 with two RBI singles. Prior to the game, he said he was feeling good with no lingering effects from the injury which kept him out during the first couple of weeks of March.</p><p><b>Luisangel Acuna</b> also started at second base and made a couple of nice plays. He was 0-for-3.</p><p><b>On deck</b>: Royals at Sox, 3:05 p.m. Sunday, Glendale, 1000-AM, <b>Noah Cameron</b> vs. <b>Chris Murphy</b>.<br></p>
entries.author: Barry M. Bloom

entries.title: White Sox might be former top prospect Jarred Kelenic's final stand
entries.summary: <p>PEORIA, Ariz. — It’s safe to say Jarred Kelenic is seeking some redemption. The No. 6 pick overall by the Mets in the 2018 draft, Kelenic had all the credentials such a lofty selection granted.</p><p>He was traded to Seattle that offseason where it certainly didn’t happen, even as the top prospect in the Mariners' organization and fourth overall in Major League Baseball.</p><p>But he had played so poorly that in mid-2023 he fractured his left foot kicking a Gatorade cooler in the home dugout out of frustration. It was then on to Atlanta where it didn’t happen, either.</p><p>He’s a .211 hitter with a .658 OPS and an 84 OPS+ — well below the major-league average of 100 – for his first five seasons. He hit .167 for the Braves last year in 60 games with an OPS+ of 49. That all speaks for itself.</p><p>The White Sox might be his final stand. Kelenic is in camp on a minor league contract and doesn’t have a spot on the club’s 40-man roster. He has to make the team. How has he weathered all the ups and downs — mostly downs?</p><p>“The game is hard,” Kelenic said. “The game sucks sometimes, man. It’ll beat you down. But at the end of the day, you’ve just got to stick to your strengths and superpowers, ride with those and have as much confidence as you can in yourself in a game where you’re set up to fail.”</p><p>Kelenic is trying, but the more things change the more they remain the same. Despite his first spring homer for the Sox on Friday — a three-run shot against the Arizona Diamondbacks at Salt River Fields — the left-handed hitting outfielder is still struggling offensively below his career clip: a .200/.238/.400 slash line with seven strikeouts in 21 plate appearances.</p><p>“It’s a small sample size. It’s Arizona,” Sox manager Will Venable said. “We’re not putting any stock into this statistically. Are you hitting the ball hard? Are you playing well defensively? Are you putting in the work? And on all that he is.”</p><p>Yet, The odds are long of him making it if Kelenic doesn’t put up some significant numbers that pop open the right eyes.</p><p>“There’s a path for him. He’s still here,” Venable added. “We haven’t made any decisions yet about him or anybody else. He still has a shot.”</p><p>At 26, the time may be coming for a career change. He was one of the highest drafted players ever from the state of Wisconsin, despite never playing ball for his Waukesha West High School team. He trained through the winter instead and decided to turn down a scholarship to play college ball at the University of Louisville after he was drafted by the Mets.</p><p>It’s been a long downhill slog ever since.</p><p>“I think just like anyone has throughout life there were a lot of things I went through that were negative,” Kelenic said. “I felt like there were really big learning opportunities for me that ultimately made me who I am today just like from an approach standpoint. The outlook of who I am, my own self.</p><p>“I can’t generalize it into one thing, but I’ve just been trying to take those times that weren’t so great and turn them into a learning opportunity because that’s the only way you can get any better.”</p><p>The Mets traded him to Seattle on Dec. 3, 2018, in the seven-player deal that netted New York aging second baseman Robinson Cano and closer Edwin Diaz. He never played a day for the Mets in the major leagues, but the Mariners were jazzed to get Kelenic. It never panned out. He batted .168 with 167 strikeouts in 147 games hose first two season, 2021 and 2022.</p><p>But perhaps there will be a new dawn for him at Camelback Ranch. Kelenic drew in a deep breath and released it when asked how he’s feeling now.</p><p>“I mean, I’m feeling pretty confident,” he said. “I’m feeling really good about where my head is at and where my swing is at. Again, it’s really early. I still need some reps to keep working on some things. There’s still some pitches I’m missing that when I’m on a roll I’m mashing those things.”</p><p>He once was the top prospect in baseball. Now, Kelenic knows better than anyone else he’s on the clock.<br></p>
entries.author: Barry M. Bloom

entries.title: Mourners honor Rev. Jesse Jackson at final funeral service: 'I love you, neighbor'
entries.summary: <p>Mourners of the Rev. Jesse Jackson gave a final goodbye to the civil rights icon for the final time before he was laid to rest Saturday, with a somber yet uplifting culmination of weeks of ceremonies that captivated thousands throughout Chicago and across the world. </p><p>Jackson, who died Feb. 16 <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/morning-edition/2026/02/17/jesse-jackson-clean-slate-act-pritzker-chicago-public-schools-preckwinkle" >at age 84</a>, was remembered as a constant, global beam of hope for justice, one that lives on through his family and his organization, the Rainbow PUSH Coalition, which hosted Saturday’s private funeral service at its Kenwood headquarters, 930 E. 50th St.</p><p>“He wanted to make sure that the world he was leaving would be better than the world he was born into,” his son U.S. Rep. Jonathan Jackson said during a rousing speech. “I want the world to know that my father believed in love, he believed in dignity of all human beings and he believed that just because you were born in the slums, the slums were never born in you.”</p><p>Jackson was interred later Saturday at <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/architecture-design/2026/03/05/rev-jesse-jackson-laid-rest-oak-woods-cemetery" >Oak Woods Cemetery</a>, 1035 E. 67th St.</p><p>Among Jackson’s many famed sentiments was his emphasis on understanding a person’s roots. “It’s not about where you are, but where you come from,” he’d say.</p><p>His youngest daughter, Ashley Jackson, recalled a road trip her father took her on when she was 15. He showed her <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/02/the-rev-jesse-jackson-greenville-south-carolina-colombia-greenville8-lying-in-state-chicago" >his childhood home in Greenville, South Carolina</a>, and sites that formed his roots in the South, like Selma, Alabama, where he first met Martin Luther King Jr.</p><p>“He wasn’t simply showing me a house. He was teaching me something about beginnings,” Ashley Jackson said. “That even when they are shadowed, they still hold light. That the distance between where you start and where you stand is sacred ground. And that what you build in that distance becomes your character.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <bsp-carousel class="Carousel Enhancement" 2x" width="300" height="300" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b44a50f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1855x1855+463+0/resize/300x300!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa2%2F05%2Faa933c2f4b33925371b3f3fdfb9b%2Fjackson-030826-062.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIzMDBweCIgd2lkdGg9IjMwMHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> </span> </div> </div> </div></div><div class="FlickityCustomNavigation-wrapper"> <div class="FlickityCustomNavigation below"> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-prev" type="button" aria-label="Previous"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-left"></use></svg> </button> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-next" type="button" aria-label="Next"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-right"></use></svg> </button> </div> </div></div> </bsp-carousel> </div> </div><p>Hundreds of people filled the chapel’s pews, which at times glowed a sunlit orange through the colorful stained glass windows.</p><p>An energetic crowd at one point shouted, “Keep hope alive,” a nod to Jackson’s famous speech <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/02/17/rev-jesse-jackson-legacy-chicago-democrats" >at the 1988 Democratic National Convention</a>. Other speakers hinted at the divisiveness in the U.S. under the Trump administrations.</p><p>Pop icon Stevie Wonder drew countless camera flashes as he performed “As,” and “They Won’t Go When I Go,” a song which he said reminded him of a time he marched with Jackson in Connecticut.</p><p>Wonder also performed a short version of the gospel song “We Shall Overcome,” an anthem of the Civil Rights Movement.</p><p>“Since I was 13, 14 years old, I’ve heard that song and I thought it was ‘we’ who should overcome,” Wonder said. “But today, it is not we who need to overcome. It is you — and you know who you are. You need to overcome hate, you need to overcome the mindset of white supremacy.”</p><p>“You shall overcome the need to dominate every single country and every people,” Wonder said. “You need to overcome gun violence, gang violence, cartel violence, government violence and domestic violence, and then this nation needs to overcome whatever fear that keeps us protecting child traffickers. Children must be protected across party lines. The innocent knows no politics.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-1a0000" name="image-1a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/25d91c2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5787x3248+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7c%2Fc3%2F290d88fd4e859bb0f1c6fbb69b6b%2Fjackson-030826-31.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/16dad38/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5787x3248+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7c%2Fc3%2F290d88fd4e859bb0f1c6fbb69b6b%2Fjackson-030826-31.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5aad309/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5787x3248+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7c%2Fc3%2F290d88fd4e859bb0f1c6fbb69b6b%2Fjackson-030826-31.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Stevie Wonder performs during the final homegoing service for Rev. Jesse Jackson at Rainbow PUSH Coalition headquarters, 930 E. 50th St., on the South Side, Saturday, March 7, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5aad309/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5787x3248+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7c%2Fc3%2F290d88fd4e859bb0f1c6fbb69b6b%2Fjackson-030826-31.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3f379e2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5787x3248+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7c%2Fc3%2F290d88fd4e859bb0f1c6fbb69b6b%2Fjackson-030826-31.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5aad309/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5787x3248+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7c%2Fc3%2F290d88fd4e859bb0f1c6fbb69b6b%2Fjackson-030826-31.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Stevie Wonder performs Saturday during the final funeral service for Rev. Jesse Jackson at Rainbow PUSH Coalition headquarters.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Comedian Chris Tucker kept the room in high spirits with a short speech about meeting Jackson when they coincidentally ended up on the same plane while traveling to a funeral. </p><p>“The Rev. told me some wisdom I’ll never forget," Tucker recalled. "He said, ‘Chris, the thing about flying like this so much, you miss the details, you miss the details.’ And I realized how much he sacrificed and gave to so many people and came and supported his people, for his people and other people and the world. He missed so much and he worked so hard,” said Tucker, who said he was forever grateful to Jackson for visiting his mother in the hospital when she dealt with a serious illness.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/03/06/rev-jesse-jackson-chicago-service-house-of-hope-pullman-neighborhood-barack-obama-jb-pritzker" >Chicago comes out in force to honor Rev. Jesse Jackson</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Jackson’s social and political impacts are well documented, but his reach was personal for Randi Johnson. “I came out today because Rev. Jesse Jackson is the reason I went to college," she said. </p><p>At 13, Johnson met Jackson for the first time at one of Rainbow PUSH’s college tours. She said she fell in love with his alma mater of North Carolina A&T and decided to get a higher education degree, eventually graduating from Lane College in Tennessee.</p><p>Sheila Clay, a 77-year-old artist, said she had many encounters with Jackson, from phone calls with him to going to New Orleans after Hurricane Katrina with him, to marching down Michigan Avenue with him. What stood out to Clay was how much he showed “divine love for his neighbor," she said. </p><p>“It touched my heart, because you don’t often hear many people just say, ‘I love you, neighbor.’”</p><p>Clay said Jackson’s fight for justice and love — which she first learned of at the age of 16 when her uncle told her about Operation Breadbasket — has always inspired her life and art.</p><p>“It wouldn’t be right not to come to say goodbye for the last time to my beautiful spirit, my Rev. Jesse Jackson,” Clay said. </p>
entries.author: Phyllis Cha

entries.title: Cubs get kick out of Japanese fan favorite Seiya Suzuki in WBC
entries.summary: <p>MESA, Ariz. — Cubs players received an added feature during their meeting before their morning workout Saturday.</p><p>They were treated to a video clip — provided by former major-league outfielder <b>Adam Jones</b> — from the outfield of the Tokyo Dome, where fans chanted the name of Cubs outfielder <b>Seiya Suzuki</b> moments before he hit one of his two home runs to lead Japan to an 8-6 victory against Korea in the World Baseball Classic.</p><p>‘‘The place goes crazy, and everybody’s hugging each other. Really cool,’’ said manager <b>Craig Counsell</b>, who got the video from Jones. ‘‘It must have been . . . a great experience, and that’s why you play the WBC — for moments like that.</p><p>‘‘These are different experiences, and this is just a life experience. And all the experiences, they mean something to you. But I think you want experiences like that.’’</p><p>Suzuki is making up for lost time since missing the 2023 WBC because of an oblique injury. He’s 3-for-7 with four runs scored in two games for Japan.</p><p>‘‘He’s been phenomenal,’’ Cubs left-hander and countryman <b>Shota Imanaga</b> said.</p><h3>No guarantees for Assad</h3><p>Right-hander <b>Javier Assad</b> pitched 3 2/3 scoreless innings in Mexico’s WBC victory Friday against Great Britain, six days after he pitched three scoreless innings and struck out four against the Dodgers.</p><p>Counsell pointed out the WBC, which is played during spring training at a high level, forces performers to prepare sooner than during a regular spring.</p><p>Assad has shown flashes of strong play during the last two seasons with the Cubs, only to be sidelined because of injuries to his right forearm and oblique. His development is valuable to the Cubs, who also are grooming right-handers <b>Colin Rea</b> and <b>Ben Brown</b> in emergency-starter roles in case of an injury to a member of their rotation.</p><p>But there’s plenty to assess entering the final 2½ weeks of spring training.</p><p>‘‘With somebody like Javy, you don’t try to figure out where they fit,’’ Counsell said. ‘‘We don’t have to do that. At some point, we’re gonna need Javy’s outs. When is probably the question.</p><p>‘‘But we want him pitching well and showing the ability to get big-league hitters out at a pretty high level. In 2024, for a couple of months, he was really one of the better starters going. You take note of things like that, and we’ve got a good version of what Javy looks like right now, the way he’s throwing the ball.’’</p><h3>Pause button on ABS success rate</h3><p>The Cubs and White Sox are at least two of several teams in the majors that have examined data in the minors in preparation for the automated ball/strike challenge system that will be implemented in the big leagues this season.</p><p>Catcher <b>Moises Ballesteros</b> has been successful on his last seven challenges, including four Wednesday against the Brewers.</p><p>But Counsell told reporters Ballesteros’ success rate of challenging pitch calls was around 50% while catching in Triple-A, where the ABS system has been used for the last couple of seasons.</p><p>‘‘It’s actually below the catchers’ average,’’ Counsell said. ‘‘My point is, I think you guys are going to say, ‘He’s good at this,’ or, ‘He’s good at that.’ And I just think we’re going to revert to the mean in this thing very quickly.’’</p><p>But is it safe to say Ballesteros, who is projected to be the Cubs’ designated hitter and third catcher, showed confidence in challenging calls?</p><p>‘‘Yeah, that’s a good point,’’ Counsell said. ‘‘I like that.’’</p><h3>Cubs 4, Athletics 3</h3><p>Left-hander <b>Shota Imanaga</b> worked exclusively on his location and was one out away from retiring his ninth consecutive batter before <b>Andy Ibanez</b> hit a high fly that eluded <b>Ian Happ</b> against the left-field fence for a double. Two batters later, <b>Brian Serven</b> clubbed a two-run home run to center. Imanaga yielded three runs and five hits in his 3 2/3-inning stint, but he didn’t walk a batter. His fastball was clocked in the 92 to 93 mph range.</p><p>• <b>Nico Hoerner</b> showed why he’s the Cubs’ most unselfish hitter and one of the most clutch hitters in the National League. He poked a two-out single down the right-field line to score <b>Josiah Hartshorn</b> and cap a two-run rally in the second.</p><p>• Infielder <b>Pedro Ramirez</b> was presented with his 2025 minor-league Gold Glove Award in a pregame ceremony, then smacked a homer to left-center against <b>Mason Barnett</b> in the second.</p><p>• <b>On deck:</b> Giants at Cubs, 3:05 p.m. Sunday, Mesa, Marquee, 104.3-FM, TBA vs. <b>Hunter Harvey</b>.<br></p>
entries.author: Mark Gonzales

entries.title: Horoscope for Sunday, March 08, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon Alert</h2> <p>There are no restrictions to shopping or important decisions today. The moon is in Scorpio. (Daylight savings begins at 2 a.m.)</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Research and behind-the-scenes discussions may benefit your home and family today. In fact, conversations about how to divide or share something — perhaps an inheritance or something related to shared property, debt or taxes — could be significant.</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is an excellent day to make future plans based on past events. Because Mercury is retrograde, do not initiate new projects; however, you can refine and improve existing ones. Be cooperative with others today for your own benefit.</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You still look magnificent in the eyes of others today. In fact, this is a good day to make your pitch about something, wrap up an old deal or rethink goals and plans. Fortunately, this is a strong day for you to recoup some kind of glitch or past mistake that can benefit you financially or in practical ways.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a positive, upbeat day! entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Fire play to 0-0 draw against Crew
entries.summary: <p>COLUMBUS, Ohio — Chris Brady finished with three saves for the Chicago Fire and Patrick Schulte stopped two shots for Columbus as the longtime rivals played to a scoreless draw in the Crew's home opener on Saturday night.</p><p>It was the 24th draw in the 80th meeting between the two clubs with the victories knotted at 28 apiece.</p><p>Five yellow cards were handed out in the final 13 minutes of regulation — three of them to the Fire (1-1-1).</p><p>Brady had two saves and Schulte wasn’t tested in the first half.</p><p>The two clubs split a pair of matches last season with both winning at home.</p><p>Columbus falls to 0-1-2 on the season.</p><p><b>Up next</b></p><p>Chicago: Hosts D.C. United on Saturday.</p><p>Columbus: Hosts Nashville SC on Saturday.<br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Dear Abby: Kind gesture blows up in an unpredictable fashion
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> Eighteen months ago, I was staying with my son and his family when they had a get-together with his wife, "Corrine's," father. His wife, Corrine's stepmother, had just finished chemo for several different types of cancer. When Corrine's dad went to leave, I offered to put together a small plate for his wife, hoping it would make her feel better.</p><p>When Corrine saw this from the outside, she came in and accused me of "giving away family food that her son might want." They have lots of money, and I replied that I just thought it would be nice for the recovering woman, and if Corrine thought she needed more food for her son, I'd be happy to get it.</p><p>My son and his wife have barely spoken to me since. I have offered to meet them for counseling and have sent my grandson little gifts every month, which have gone unacknowledged. I saw them for the first time last month at a family wedding. They barely said a word, and my son avoided me. The rest of the family could not believe how rude they were to me.</p><p>I don't know what else to do. I have reached out. I've written notes. When I talk with my son on the phone, he's receptive. Then he talks to his wife, and all of a sudden, nothing is acceptable. Please help. — <i>ESTRANGED IN CALIFORNIA</i></p><p><b>DEAR ESTRANGED:</b> Could there be more behind this than you have mentioned — perhaps hurt feelings that drove Corinne over the edge? You have done all you can to make up with your super-sensitive daughter-in-law (who appears to be uniquely possessive of her food). Take a step back. Sometimes, things get better with time, and that is what I am hoping will happen for you.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> A year ago, I got out of a verbally abusive relationship. Five months later, I met the man of my dreams, who had also been in a very bad marriage. We are really good together, but if something bad happens, he flies off the handle and accuses me of being stupid and not normal. He also threatens to leave me if I don't change my behavior. This was the same threat I got from my ex. I suspect that when I get upset about a problem, he thinks I'm his ex-wife. What should I do? I love him, but I don't want to be back in the same kind of relationship as before. — <i>SCARED AND CONFUSED IN MICHIGAN</i></p><p><b>DEAR SCARED:</b> You may not want to be back in the same kind of relationship that you had before, but it looks like that's almost exactly where you are. I don't blame you for being scared and confused because there is something wrong with your manpicker. You have now been with two emotional abusers. Before involving yourself in any more romantic relationships, it's important that you discuss this with a psychologist or other licensed mental health professional who can help you break this pattern. If you do, you'll spare yourself years of bad choices and heartache.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>Good advice for everyone — teens to seniors — is in "The Anger in All of Us and How to Deal With It." To order, send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby, Anger Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Girls run the court at Chicago Bulls' annual clinic
entries.summary: <p>White-and-orange WNBA basketballs cut through the air inside the Chicago Bulls training facility Saturday as the squeak of sneakers and the thump of dribbling echoed across the hardwood.</p><p>About 150 girls moved through drills when Beyoncé’s anthem filled the gym: “Who run the world? Girls.”</p><p>The clinic was part of the Chicago Bulls’ and Gatorade’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls/2025/03/05/bulls-host-2nd-annual-girls-day-of-play-for-girls-to-understand-theres-a-place-for-them-in-sports" >third annual</a> Girls’ Day of Play, a Women’s History Month event aimed at introducing basketball to girls aged eight to 18 in an inclusive environment. It also marked the culmination of a six-month coaching initiative that provides 50 Chicago-area coaches with resources to support female-identifying athletes throughout their seasons.</p><p>“We were trying to figure out how we, as a men's professional team, address the rates of girls dropping out or not even joining sports,” said Adrienne Scherenzel, Bulls vice president of community engagement. “We can’t get to all the girls, but we know we can get to the coaches.”</p><p>The coaching development program partners with the nonprofit Center for Healing and Justice Through Sport, along with Bulls youth hoops coaches, to train Chicago-area coaches on how to better encourage, support and develop their athletes.</p><p>Scherenzel said the program is designed to respond to questions coaches have throughout their seasons. For example, some male coaches have asked how to better support athletes during menstrual cycles, prompting the program to provide care packages with menstrual products.</p><p>“It's a nice, discreet, black case, so they can take it with them, and just have it on the bus, or in their first aid kit,” Scherenzel said, “so if girls need products, they can always get them there.”</p><div in Rogers Park for three seasons, but this was her first year participating in the Bulls’ development program.</p><p>“I changed my coaching strategy because of this program,” she said.</p><p>Blagaich said she used to start every season by focusing heavily on strategy. Now her priority is building confidence and skills before diving into the playbook.</p><p>“When I first started playing basketball, I was given that binder of plays — X's and O's — and I knew as a player I needed to learn this,” she said. “I changed my coaching strategy and focused more on skill building and overall goals, which allowed my players to be more confident on the court."</p><p>One of Blagaich’s athletes, first-year point guard Lucie Pauni, said she felt even more confident after the clinic and plans to bring what she learned back to her team.</p><p>“I'll teach them how to data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b729d52/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F84%2Faf%2F8a8280194d128c4dc04df3f97503%2Fbullsdop-260308-008.jpg" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Scenes from the Chicago Bulls’ “Girls’ Play Day” on March 7, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Arthur Maiorella/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Kerasia Ntokos of suburban Cass Junior High said the girls-only clinic was “less nerve-wracking” than a co-ed Bulls clinic she attended in the past.</p><p>“It's really interesting to see so many girls who love the same sport in one room,” she said.</p><p>Ntokos said the coaches were empowering while also pushing her to improve.</p><p>“Even if you don't think you're the best basketball player, or you just like the sport in general, come and try it out,” Ntokos said. “I bet when you walk out of here, you'll be like, 10 times more confident, because I definitely was.”</p><p></p><p>Athletes and coaches alike also took note of DJ Hillery Banks, who mixed artists like Olivia Dean and The Cheetah Girls with Chicago house music to keep the energy high. The Bulls selected Banks as one of three Black DJs representing the future of Chicago’s Black music community last month.</p><p>“She's awesome – the energy is so upbeat,” Ntokos said.</p><p>The Chicago Sky’s mascot, Skye the Lioness, also made rounds around the gym, running drills and snapping photos with athletes.</p><p>When the event ended, girls and coaches left the Bulls training facility sweaty, energized and accompanied by some new friends.</p>
entries.author: Somer Van Benton | WBEZ

entries.title: Labeled a gang member and deported, longtime Chicagoan tries to restart in his Mexican hometown
entries.summary: <p>Francisco Gonzalez-Jasso, a longtime Little Village resident, didn't know exactly how to make his way back to his childhood home in Torreón, a city in Mexico's northern state of Cahuila. He hadn’t been there in 33 years.</p><p>“I kept asking people on the bus, 'Is Torreón next?' ” Gonzalez-Jasso, 55, says in his native Spanish.</p><p>In Torreón, he took a cab to his old block. But the years had erased his memory of the house. He paced the street, checking the addresses on every door. When he found the right one, he says, the house looked different. It was really small.</p><p>“I started yelling out my brother’s name: Cesar, Cesar,” Gonzalez-Jasso says. “Once I saw his face, I told the taxi driver to leave. Then, I embraced my brother.</p><p>“I was broken.".</p><p>The last time they were together, Cesar, now 50, was 17.</p><p>Gonzalez-Jasso is trying to rebuild his life in Mexico after he was arrested by Border Patrol agents last October in Little Village in front of friends and neighbors. He was deported a few days later. WBEZ has been keeping up with Gonzalez-Jasso after visiting him in Mexico in December, not long after he arrived there.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Gonzalez-Jasso has breakfast at his brother's house in February." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/89872ab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F91%2F700b43da45b4b6210841916112e5%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0172.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d8639d5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F91%2F700b43da45b4b6210841916112e5%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0172.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/89872ab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa8%2F91%2F700b43da45b4b6210841916112e5%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0172.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Gonzalez-Jasso has breakfast at his brother’s house in February. He has a large extended family in his hometown but the adjustment back home has been a struggle.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Duilio Rodríguez/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The tall man with a sturdy build was a well-known presence in his Chicago neighborhood for more than three decades. Still, despite not having a criminal record, the U.S. Department of Homeland Security branded him a member of the Latin Kings gang — a tag that has followed him to Mexico.</p><p>Gonzalez-Jasso is among hundreds of people swept up during Operation Midway Blitz, the aggressive Trump administration deportation campaign aimed at clearing “violent criminals” off Chicago-area streets. He also is among immigrants who say they were wrongly accused of being a criminal or having ties to gangs.</p><p>“It’s humiliating for me,” Gonzalez-Jasso says. “I’m not a criminal. I never harmed anyone.”</p><h3>‘This was his home’ </h3><p>Gonzalez-Jasso was known on 26th Street in Little Village as Panchito. His arrest and deportation last fall were a gut punch to neighbors who remember him as a friendly face always ready to lend a hand.</p><p>“I cried, honestly, I cried when I learned that Panchito was arrested,” Carlos Macías, the owner of Carniceria y Taqueria Aguas Calientes on 26th street, said in Spanish. The two have been friends for more than 20 years.</p><p>Also speaking Spanish, Ana Guerrero, a waitress at the <i>taqueria</i> who often served Gonzalez-Jasso, said: “He was like your daily coffee, always here in the mornings before heading to work. He was always very respectful. This was his home.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-960000" name="image-960000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7f3cefd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F32%2F29%2Fa9417bc546409b94b56c67de1c31%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0451.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/09aa641/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F32%2F29%2Fa9417bc546409b94b56c67de1c31%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0451.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7f3cefd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+190/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F32%2F29%2Fa9417bc546409b94b56c67de1c31%2Fpanchito-duilior-dsc0451.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Gonzalez-Jasso carries a little bit of Chicago with him in his hometown in Mexico, where he misses the life he built over three decades in Chicago. He also has Cubs and Blackhawks’ gear.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Duilio Rodríguez/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The afternoon of his arrest, Gonzalez-Jasso says, he and his friends were having a birthday party at Jacaranda, a bar on 26th Street. He stepped out to buy a pack of cigarettes. That's when Border Patrol agents arrested him.</p><p>“The saddest thing, and it hurts, is that they grabbed me, they forced me down, pointed a gun at me, in front of people, children.” Gonzalez-Jasso says. “Your world collapses. Your life is over. Tied up, handcuffed, in pain, disappointed. What did I do?”</p><p>He says Border Patrol agents punched him several times as they dragged him to a van. The agents didn’t know his name, didn’t show him a warrant and had no reason to stop him, he says. Video of his arrest circulated on <a class="Link" href="https://www.facebook.com/100001704351957/videos/1482298379669220/" target="_blank" ><u>social media</u></a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Gonzalez-Jasso shows the video of his arrest by federal agents in Chicago. The video circulated on social media, spreading word of his arrest and deportation before he arrived back home.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Duilio Rodriguez/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>DHS released a statement that afternoon saying, “U.S. Border Patrol conducted targeted enforcement resulting in the arrest of Francisco Gonzalez-Jasso, a criminal alien from Mexico and member of the Latin Kings criminal organization.”</p><p>The agency hasn't responded to questions about what evidence it has of that claim.</p><p>Gonzalez-Jasso’s name isn’t listed in state or city records as having gang ties or a criminal record. Nor was he included in the 2018 Chicago Crime Commission “Gang Book,” the most recent one published.</p><p>He lived in Chicago without legal immigration status and was deported once before, in the 1980s. But he says the government accusations naming him as a gang member aren’t true. He worked in construction for years and in places that required background checks including school buildings.</p><p>“I would have been caught already after 30 years,” he says.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-6c0000" name="module-6c0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-descriptions="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-2d0001" name="list-2d0001"></a><div class="PageList-header" data-no-title> <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > </div> </div> <div class="PageListH-items" data-with-borders data-list-loadmore-items> <div class="PageListH-items-item"><div class="PagePromoB"> <div class="PagePromo-media"><a class="Link" aria-label="She was deported to Mexico. He’s in detention. Now, this Chicago couple has to figure out what’s next." href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/12/28/deported-chicago-to-mexico-husband-ice-detention-middway-blitz" ><picture DHS has made other accusations — and filed charges against some, only to drop them later for lack of evidence.</p><p>After his arrest, Gonzalez-Jasso says, he was taken to the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement processing center in Broadview, where officials “treated animals better than us.”</p><p>There wasn’t enough food or water, he says, and he was denied his medicine. He was miserable in a crowded cell. Fearful he would be stuck there for weeks, Gonzalez-Jasso gave up and signed his deportation papers. He says he was sent to detention centers in four states before arriving in Matamoros, Mexico.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>On Gonzalez-Jasso’s bedside table is a portrait of his parents. He was unable to see them after he migrated to the United States more than 30 years ago and they are now deceased.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Duilio Rodríguez/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Word of his deportation made it to Mexico first </h3><p>Gonzalez-Jasso arrived in his hometown in bad shape. He hadn’t slept, showered or changed clothes in days. He was in pain from the punches during his arrest, he says.</p><p>He spent two days in bed. It took him several weeks to feel like himself again, he says.</p><p>“I kept thinking, ‘What am I going to do,’” he says. “I was deported like the worst criminal. My reputation is gone.”</p><p>And the home where he was now living wasn’t what he remembered. Over the years, he had lost both his parents and two brothers. The faded curtains and worn furniture were a constant reminder that three decades had passed, that his parents were no longer there to hold things together.</p><p>Gonzalez-Jasso says he felt like an intruder there despite his brother's efforts to make him feel welcome. Cesar was living there with his wife and three children.</p><p>His brother and other relatives had seen the videos of his arrest even before he arrived — making his integration that much harder. When his sister heard of his arrest, she was so shaken she almost had a nervous breakdown.</p><p>“I didn’t want anyone to know I was thrown out like that,” Gonzalez-Jasso says.</p><div </div> </div> </div></div><div class="FlickityCustomNavigation-wrapper"> <div class="FlickityCustomNavigation below"> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-prev" type="button" aria-label="Previous"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-left"></use></svg> </button> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-next" type="button" aria-label="Next"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-right"></use></svg> </button> </div> </div></div> </bsp-carousel> </div> </div><p>Four months after his deportation, Gonzalez-Jasso is living on a modest pension from his construction work. He shipped his clothes from Chicago and gave away the furniture he left behind. He is trying to get someone to haul his two cars.</p><p>He’s keeping busy remodeling his parents’ home, doing a complete overhaul of the kitchen and the bedrooms.</p><p>But he worries about the violence in Mexico. One of his brothers disappeared years ago and was never found.</p><p>Once in a while, he replays the video of his arrest. It’s painful to hear the sounds — the whistles, a woman yelling <i>“la migra”</i> — and to see himself surrounded by Border Patrol agents.</p><p>Gonzalez-Jasso wants to clear his name so everyone knows he’s not a gang member. But he doesn’t know where to start.</p><p>And he misses his Little Village friends and neighbors. Some of them recently traveled to Mexico to see him for a few days. They aren't his blood relatives. But they were his family.</p><p>In Chicago, he says, his voice cracking, “There are many people who love me. And I miss them.”</p>
entries.author: Adriana Cardona-Maguigad |  WBEZ

entries.title: Jesse Jackson’s hometown determined to preserve its rich shared history amid rapid change
entries.summary: <p>GREENVILLE, S.C. — Black children in Greenville couldn’t wait to grow up and go to Sterling High School, many graduates recall. Now what remains are memories of its majorettes and its championship sports and its excellent, committed teachers.</p><p>A young Jesse Jackson shone there, as a three-letter athlete who earned a football scholarship, as an activist and as an all-around student. Known to set hearts on campus aflutter, he graced many a page in the 1959 Torch yearbook published by the county’s oldest Black high school.</p><p>On an unofficial tour of “Jesse Jackson” landmarks, there’s not much left to see of the school,<b> </b>which mysteriously<b> </b>burned in 1967.</p><p>There also are few markers to highlight historic integration and civil rights efforts made by Jackson and others that altered Greenville's history, current leaders say, and continue to shape it today.</p><p>Jackson’s home state on Monday granted him a hero’s welcome, acknowledging him as a son of South Carolina. It cordoned off its grand Capitol rotunda for a lying in honor so thousands of<b> </b>ordinary citizens could pay their respects to one of the most influential Americans to come from the Palmetto State. His casket processed through the streets, drawn by horses, and flags were dropped to half staff.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a90000" name="image-a90000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/48419b3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2d%2Feb%2Fb4e58d954d7893d34958ce426009%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-10.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/98f6743/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2d%2Feb%2Fb4e58d954d7893d34958ce426009%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-10.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/682e145/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2d%2Feb%2Fb4e58d954d7893d34958ce426009%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-10.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A horse-drawn caisson with the Rev. Jesse Jackson's casket arrives to lay in honor at the South Carolina Capitol on March 2." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/682e145/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2d%2Feb%2Fb4e58d954d7893d34958ce426009%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-10.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/36f304c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2d%2Feb%2Fb4e58d954d7893d34958ce426009%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-10.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/682e145/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2d%2Feb%2Fb4e58d954d7893d34958ce426009%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-10.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A horse-drawn caisson with the Rev. Jesse Jackson’s casket arrives to lay in honor at the South Carolina Capitol on March 2.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Now his rapidly gentrifying hometown is discussing how to extend honors to their most famous son and the rich legacy that surrounded him.</p><p>“South Carolina has stepped up to the plate, and everything I've seen, I've just been very, very impressed,” says Peggy Baxter, fellow Sterling graduate and a key figure with Jackson in organizing to get Greenville County to adopt the federal Martin Luther King birthday holiday.</p><p>Many folks in Greenville claim ties to the internationally-known Jackson, who died on Feb 17 at age 84, but his contemporaries are getting on in years and passing on.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a It is now part of the Sterling Community Center. Plans are in the works for a green space behind the center and a memorial for Jackson.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>So they’re mapping out a Sterling Community Memorial Grove, a $2.3 million project that will add pecan wood benches, walking paths and trees to green up the space behind a community center that was once the high school’s gym, along with a torch monument — a nod to the yearbook — and a memorial for Jackson.</p><p>“So people will not forget Sterling,” says Jameka Murphy, of the Sterling Land Trust, aimed at preserving access to the surrounding historically Black neighborhood for longtime families who might otherwise be priced out if the homes were redeveloped. “They can come and be like, ‘Yeah, this is where I went to school, I remember this, and the dances,” says Murphy.</p><p>Sitting in a bright hallway inside the community center that’s papered in yearbook pages and school photographs, Baxter says she graduated in the gym in the 1950s — and now is a regular at the center’s senior citizen programs. Tiger faces painted on the gym floor and carved into a metal bike rack out front hearkens back to Sterling’s Tigers mascot.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A plaque commemorates the old Sterling High School, outside what is now the Sterling Community Center. Jesse Jackson graduated from in 1959 from Sterling, considered the county’s oldest Black high school.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“There's still a really great spirit with some of the alums now in remembering Sterling and wanting to do great things in terms of this community and really bringing improvement, because it really took a nosedive, and it's coming back,” says Baxter, a Sterling Land Trust board member.</p><p>Across one street from the community center, crews banged on the frame of a future McMansion. Across another, new houses signaled price tags that eclipse the older homes with their trendy black window frames.</p><p>“Even just in the last five years, it changed a lot,” Murphy says of the neighborhood where a teenaged Jackson could walk to school from the family home where his beautician mother did hair. “And I think the older people, they don't want so much change. They want people to remember what it used to be and what that emotion was like when they went here because they felt like they already lost a lot in the fire.”</p><p>The building boom follows what’s been happening in other legacy Black neighborhoods, including Haynie-Sirrine, where Jackson’s mother birthed him inside a tiny one-story home that at the time had no indoor bathroom. Today the modest house is one of three stark outliers amid new construction.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-4c0000" name="image-4c0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2938060/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0b%2F70%2F5882838d408f83196a04998843a6%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-106.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/78009a0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0b%2F70%2F5882838d408f83196a04998843a6%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-106.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0bc9b48/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0b%2F70%2F5882838d408f83196a04998843a6%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-106.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A sign showing a street named in honor of the Rev. Jackson, Jr. Street,” signs that had been approved in 2022. The housing projects where he lived in his youth, named for him in the 1980s, have since been demolished.</p><p>There’s another plaque in town that honors Jackson, “probably the only marker with his name on it,” as longtime City Council member Lillian Brock Flemming says.</p><p>It dates back to one of the times older folks remember Jackson returning to town in 1967 to lend his organizing power as director of Operation Breadbasket after moving away in 1964, landing eventually and permanently in Chicago.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> Jesse Jackson brought Dr. Martin Luther King to Greenville in 1967 to work together to take on discrimination at The Claussen Bakery.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Amid a strike at the Claussen Bakery protesting discrimination against Black workers in hiring practices and promotions, Jackson showed up with his influential friend and mentor, Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., and pulled together a march.</p><p>“Because of that march, it did not take Claussen’s Bakery long, they began to hire Blacks for truck drivers, Blacks to work in the store and other high level employees,” Flemming recalled.</p><p>Claussen’s Bakery appears on the Black history tour touted on Greenville’s<b> </b>website.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-9a0000" name="image-9a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275"
